1: revelation of God Bless the LORD, O my soul! O LORD my God, you are very great! You are clothed with splendor and majesty, God clothed in splendor and majesty — the Transfiguration discloses this glory: his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light (Matthew 17:2). The resurrection appearance radiates the same divine splendor: his appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow (Matthew 28:3). The great God clothed in majesty is the Christ who was clothed in human flesh and then revealed his hidden glory.
2: revelation of God covering yourself with light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent. God clothed in light — God is light, and in him is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). Christ: I am the light of the world (John 8:12). The stretching out of the heavens is the creative act of the Word: in the beginning was the Word... all things were made through him (John 1:1,3). The tent stretched out is the tabernacle of the Incarnation — he pitched his tent among us (John 1:14).
3: shadow He lays the beams of his chambers on the waters; he makes the clouds his chariot; he rides on the wings of the wind; God riding clouds and wind — Christ coming on the clouds: they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory (Matthew 24:30; Revelation 1:7 — behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him). The cloud-rider of Ps 104 is identified in the NT as the returning Christ.
4: direct he makes his messengers winds, his ministers a flame of fire. Hebrews 1:7 quotes this verse directly to contrast angels with the Son: Of the angels he says, He makes his angels winds, and his ministers a flame of fire. But of the Son he says, Your throne, O God, is forever and ever. The angels who are like wind and fire are servants; the Son is the eternal God enthroned. Psalms 104:4 establishes the angel-servant status that proves the Son's superiority.
5: shadow He set the earth on its foundations, so that it should never be moved. The earth's foundations set by Christ the creator: by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible (Colossians 1:16). Hebrews 1:10-12 quotes Psalms 102:25-27 similarly — you, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning — applying it to the Son. The one who set the earth's foundation now holds all things together (Colossians 1:17).
6: shadow You covered it with the deep as with a garment; the waters stood above the mountains. The primordial waters — Genesis creation imagery that John echoes in the new creation. The Spirit hovering over the waters (Genesis 1:2) is the Spirit Christ sends (John 20:22). The deep covered by waters becomes the deep that Christ commands (Mark 4:39) and the deep of death he descends into and rises from (Romans 10:7).
7: shadow At your rebuke they fled; at the sound of your thunder they took to flight. Waters fleeing at God's rebuke — Christ rebuking the storm: he rebuked the wind and the raging waves, and they ceased, and there was a great calm (Luke 8:24). The waters of chaos that fled at creation's command flee again at the voice of the incarnate Creator. The disciples' question — who then is this? — is answered by Psalm 104.
8: shadow The mountains rose, the valleys sank down to the place that you appointed for them. Mountains rising and valleys sinking — the ordering of creation by the Word. The eschatological reversal: every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be made low (Luke 3:5, citing Isaiah 40:4) — the preparation for the coming of the Lord. The creator who raised mountains will level them for his own appearing.
9: shadow You set a boundary that they may not pass, so that they might not again cover the earth. The boundary set for the waters — the covenant with creation (Genesis 9:11). Christ fulfills the Noah sign: the rainbow covenant (Genesis 9:13-17) is the type of the new covenant in his blood. He who set the boundary for the seas is the one who says of the church: the gates of hell shall not prevail against it (Matthew 16:18) — another boundary that holds.
10: revelation of God You make springs gush forth in the valleys; they flow between the hills; Springs in the valleys — the living water Christ gives: the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). The creator God who makes springs gush in the valley is the same Lord who offers the Samaritan woman water that springs up to eternal life. Creation's provision is a parable of the gospel.
11: shadow they give drink to every beast of the field; the wild donkeys quench their thirst. Every creature given drink by God — the providential care that points to Christ's universal provision. Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them (Matthew 6:26). The God who quenches the wild donkey's thirst is the Father Christ reveals, who cares for every creature.
12: shadow Beside them the birds of the heavens dwell; they sing among the branches. Birds singing in the branches — the imagery Christ uses for the kingdom: the kingdom of heaven is like a grain of mustard seed... it becomes a tree, so that the birds of the air come and make nests in its branches (Matthew 13:31-32). The birds singing in creation's branches are the type of the nations finding rest in the kingdom of God.
13: shadow From your lofty abode you water the mountains; the earth is satisfied with the fruit of your work. Earth satisfied by God's fruit — the spiritual fruitfulness Christ produces: I chose you and appointed you that you should go and bear fruit and that your fruit should abide (John 15:16). The satisfied earth of Ps 104 is the type of the new creation where Christ's work produces permanent fruit that abides forever.
14: shadow You cause the grass to grow for the livestock and plants for man to cultivate, that he may bring forth food from the earth God providing food through creation — fulfilled in Christ the bread of life. The grass-and-grain provision of Ps 104 is good; Christ surpasses it: your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died... I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever (John 6:49-51).
15: shadow and wine to gladden the heart of man, oil to make his face shine and bread to strengthen man's heart. Wine, oil, and bread — the eucharistic elements. Christ transforms ordinary wine (John 2:1-11 — the first miracle) and bread (the feeding miracles; the Last Supper) into the sacramental signs of the new covenant. The oil suggests anointing; Christ the Anointed One (Messiah) is himself the oil that makes the face shine (Psalms 45:7 — anointed with the oil of gladness).
16: shadow The trees of the LORD are watered abundantly, the cedars of Lebanon that he planted. Trees planted and watered by the LORD — the righteous flourishing like cedars (Psalms 92:12). Christ plants the church (John 15:1-5 — I am the vine, you are the branches) and waters it through the Spirit. The tree planted by the LORD is the image of the disciple rooted in Christ, bearing fruit in season (Psalms 1:3; John 15:16).
17: shadow In them the birds build their nests; the stork has her home in the fir trees. Nesting birds finding home in God's trees — the kingdom parable again (Matthew 13:31-32). Those who find their home in Christ find shelter in the one who is simultaneously the tree and the Creator who planted the tree. The nesting place is the abiding of John 15:4-5.
18: shadow The high mountains are for the wild goats; the rocks are a refuge for the rock badgers. Rocky refuge for small creatures — God's providential care for the vulnerable. Christ: not one sparrow will fall to the ground apart from your Father (Matthew 10:29). Every creature finding its refuge in the created order is a parable of every soul finding refuge in Christ the Rock (1 Corinthians 10:4).
19: shadow He made the moon to mark the seasons; the sun knows its time for setting. Seasons and times ordered by God — the eschatological times ordered by Christ. It is not for you to know times or seasons that the Father has fixed by his own authority (Acts 1:7). The creator who set the moon and sun's times has fixed the day of Christ's return; as the sun knows its time, so the Son knows when the Father will send him again.
20: shadow You make darkness, and it is night, when all the beasts of the forest creep about. Darkness and night — the realm of the enemy that Christ invaded. The night is coming, when no one can work (John 9:4); the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it (John 1:5). Christ is the light that came into the world's night; the beasts that creep in darkness are routed by his presence.
21: shadow The young lions roar for their prey, seeking their food from God. Even predators seeking food from God — the providential care extended even to the violent. Christ: he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust (Matthew 5:45). The common grace that feeds even lions is the goodness of the God Christ reveals — who sends rain on the fields of those who crucified him.
22: shadow When the sun rises, they steal away and lie down in their dens. Darkness and light alternating — the rhythm of creation that Christ transforms. He is the light that makes the darkness retreat permanently: the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb (Revelation 21:23). The day-and-night rhythm ends in the new creation where there is no night.
23: shadow Man goes out to his work and to his labor until the evening. Human labor until evening — the workday Christ inhabited. He was a carpenter (Mark 6:3) who worked with his hands; in his ministry he said I must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming, when no one can work (John 9:4). The daily labor of the human being is the creation order Christ dignified by entering it.
24: revelation of God O LORD, how manifold are your works! In wisdom you have made them all; the earth is full of your creatures. Creation made in wisdom — Christ is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24; Colossians 2:3 — in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge). Paul: for by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible (Colossians 1:16). The manifold works of God are the works of the Son through whom and for whom all things exist (Colossians 1:16-17).
25: shadow Here is the sea, great and wide, which teems with creatures innumerable, living things both small and great. The teeming sea — the harvest field of Christ's disciples. Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men (Matthew 4:19). The sea teeming with creatures is the world teeming with people for whom Christ died; the net of the kingdom (Matthew 13:47-50) gathers from this sea.
26: shadow There go the ships, and Leviathan, which you formed to play in it. Leviathan playing in the sea — the chaos monster tamed and made a plaything of God. Christ conquers the ultimate Leviathan: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him (Colossians 2:15). The serpent-dragon of Revelation (Revelation 12:9; Psalms 20:2 — the ancient serpent, who is the devil) is defeated by the Lamb.
27: shadow These all look to you, to give them their food in due season. All creation looking to God for provision — the posture Christ taught: look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not of more value than they? (Matthew 6:26). The looking of every creature to God is the posture of faith Christ calls humans to adopt toward the Father.
28: shadow When you give it to them, they gather it up; when you open your hand, they are filled with good things. God opening his hand to fill — the feeding miracles of Christ. He opens his hands in the wilderness (Matthew 14:19 — taking the loaves, looking to heaven, blessing and breaking) and fills five thousand. He opens his hands on the cross to fill all who come to him: Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters (Isaiah 55:1).
29: shadow When you hide your face, they are dismayed; when you take away their breath, they die and return to their dust. The hidden face causing death — Christ's experience at the cross: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matthew 27:46). The hiding of the divine face and the withdrawal of breath are the depths Christ entered on behalf of all who face the dismay of dying. His death exhausts the dismay so that his people face death with the Spirit's breath within them.
30: direct When you send forth your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the face of the ground. The Spirit sent to create and renew — Pentecost and the new creation. Christ breathes the Spirit on his disciples (John 20:22 — he breathed on them and said, Receive the Holy Spirit). The Spirit sent at Pentecost renews the face of the earth by creating the new covenant community: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17). The renewing Spirit of Psalms 104:30 is the Spirit of Christ.
31: shadow May the glory of the LORD endure forever; may the LORD rejoice in his works, The enduring glory of the LORD — Christ's glory that endures: the glory that you have given me I have given to them (John 17:22). The LORD who rejoices in his works rejoices in the new creation accomplished through Christ — the joy that was set before him (Hebrews 12:2) for which Christ endured the cross. Creation's joy and God's joy meet in the resurrection of the Son.
32: revelation of God who looks on the earth and it trembles, who touches the mountains and they smoke! The earth trembling at God's look — the earthquake and darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51-54), the earthquake at the resurrection (Matthew 28:2). The earth recognizes its Creator even when humanity does not. The centurion's response — truly this was the Son of God! — is the human echo of the earth's trembling recognition.
33: theme I will sing to the LORD as long as I live; I will sing praise to my God while I have being. Singing to God as long as I live — the eternal praise of those whose life is Christ: to live is Christ, and to die is gain (Philippians 1:21). The praise that lasts as long as life lasts becomes, in Christ, eternal — for I am the resurrection and the life... whoever lives and believes in me shall never die (John 11:25-26). Endless living means endless singing.
34: theme May my meditation be pleasing to him, for I rejoice in the LORD. Meditation pleasing to God — the prayer Christ taught: meditation on God's word and works, grounded in the one who is himself the Word (John 1:1). Whatever is true... honorable... just... pure... lovely... commendable... think about these things (Philippians 4:8). The meditation pleasing to God has Christ as its ultimate content and source.
35: shadow Let sinners be consumed from the earth, and let the wicked be no more! Bless the LORD, O my soul! Praise the LORD! Sinners consumed and the wicked no more — the eschatological cleansing of the new creation: nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who does what is detestable or false, but only those who are written in the Lamb's book of life (Revelation 21:27). The final Praise the LORD! (Hallelujah) is the word of Revelation 19:1-6 at the consummation — all creation joining the Hallelujah! of Ps 104's closing word.
